quotes: my experience is pretty honed in on Novedia right now, I am the original unpaid intern
it was a quick slack message over to Shawnee
she got back to me and said, you know what, you can handle that by yourself
role_fit_and_relevant_experience.rationale: The candidate has direct, hands-on experience reviewing K-12 lesson content in what appears to be the exact platform (Novodia/CG3) this role involves, which is meaningful. However, the role is described as an unpaid internship, suggesting limited formal QA experience. There is no mention of prior QA roles, educational content review experience outside this context, or broader background that would demonstrate the core bar has been met independently of this single internship. The experience is real but narrow and junior.

quotes: making sure the activity type matches the questions
the question was based on this image, what's the answer, and the image is behind all of the multiple choice answers
making sure the age of the students on the screen, match the age of the students working through the screen
a head faced the correct way on a coin, you know, right or left
domain_judgment.rationale: The candidate demonstrates genuine instinct for spotting content and UX errors in educational materials — catching an activity-type/image visibility mismatch is a real, substantive QA catch that shows understanding of how students interact with content. Attention to detail on image accuracy (coin orientation), audio alignment, and age-appropriateness of on-screen characters shows solid domain awareness. The example is concrete and shows they understand the student experience lens. Slightly below a 4 because the range of error types described, while real, is somewhat limited to visual/UI issues rather than deeper content accuracy (factual, curriculum alignment, etc.).

quotes: I like to start at the top, go straight down, you know, top to bottom, left to right, default to tier 3
I like to make sure that I keep my list of known bugs next to me
I like to do 30-10 split, so I'll work for 30 minutes, if I run into an issue, I'll work on that issue for 10 minutes
I'll have written down everything I've tried to see if it worked or not on various issues
process_and_methodology.rationale: The candidate describes a structured, repeatable review process: familiarize with style/context first, review top-to-bottom, maintain a known-issues checklist, and use a timed 30-10 problem-solving loop before escalating. The 30-10 framework is a genuine personal methodology for managing unknowns and escalation decisions. The approach to bug triage (try yourself, then Slack, then ticket, then wait overnight for self-resolving issues) is practical and shows process thinking. Loses a point because the description of how to write up a bug ticket for others to act on is absent — the question specifically asked about ticket writing quality, which was not addressed.

quotes: so yeah, I like to make sure that I keep my list next to me, my woods about me
you know, top to bottom, left to right, default to tier 3
so, yeah, I'm Sam Carver, and I look forward to hearing from you guys
communication.rationale: The candidate communicates conversationally and the substance is generally followable, but the delivery is notably rambling with frequent filler phrases, incomplete thoughts, and tangents. Key ideas are buried rather than led with. The answer to question 3 in particular drifts into the 30-10 methodology without ever directly addressing how to write a bug ticket. While informal delivery alone is not penalized, the lack of organization makes it harder to extract clear answers and suggests the candidate would need development in professional written/verbal communication for a role that requires clear documentation.

quotes: the next question is kind of a perfect example
I'm Sam Carver, and I look forward to hearing from you guys
instruction_following_and_professionalis.rationale: The candidate addressed all three questions, maintained a professional tone throughout, and completed the submission. The response is self-aware about the question structure (explicitly transitioning between questions). Minor deduction because the answer to question 3 (bug ticket writing) was only partially addressed — the escalation decision process was covered but the mechanics of writing a clear, actionable ticket were not described.

overall_summary: Sam Carver is a current unpaid intern at Novodia with direct, platform-specific QA experience reviewing K-12 lessons. Their strongest showing is in domain judgment — the activity-type/image visibility catch is a concrete, meaningful example of real QA instinct. Their process is structured (top-to-bottom review, known-issues checklist, 30-10 escalation loop) and shows genuine personal methodology. The main gaps are: (1) limited breadth of experience beyond this single internship context, (2) failure to address how to write an actionable bug ticket despite being directly asked, and (3) communication that is conversational to the point of being disorganized. This candidate appears to be a developing QA reviewer with real potential but not yet at a fully independent level. Confidence is moderate — the transcript is complete but the informal delivery makes it harder to assess the ceiling of their capability.

limitations: Transcription quality may affect scoring accuracy.
Scores reflect rubric criteria only, not holistic assessment.
